But generating all possible x^i and y^j could be computationally intensive if done naively, especially since x and y can be up to 100 and bound up to 1e6.

vector<int> powerfulIntegers(int x, int y, int bound) {

    // Generate A and B
    vector<long long> A = generate_powers(x, bound);
    vector<long long> B = generate_powers(y, bound);

    unordered_set<int> result;

solution:
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <unordered_set>
#include <algorithm>

using namespace std;

vector<long long> generate_powers(int base, int bound) {
    vector<long long> res;
    if (base == 1) {
        res.push_back(1);
        return res;
    }
    long long current = 1;
    res.push_back(current);
    while (true) {
        current *= base;
        if (current > bound) {
            break;
        }
        res.push_back(current);
    }
    return res;
}

vector<int> powerfulIntegers(int x, int y, int bound) {
    vector<long long> A = generate_powers(x, bound);
    vector<long long> B = generate_powers(y, bound);
    
    unordered_set<int> result;
    
    for (long long a : A) {
        if (a > bound) continue;
        int target = bound - a;
        if (target < 1) continue;
        
        auto it = upper_bound(B.begin(), B.end(), target);
        int idx = it - B.begin() - 1;
        
        if (idx >= 0) {
            for (int j = 0; j <= idx; ++j) {
                long long s = a + B[j];
                if (s <= bound) {
                    result.insert(s);
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
    return vector<int>(result.begin(), result.end());
}
```

efficiency: HIGH

time complexity: O((log x + log y) * (log x + log y))

space complexity: O(m + n + k), where m and n are the sizes of A and B, and k is the number of unique sums.

energy implications: LOW

explanation: The solution efficiently generates all possible x^i and y^j values up to the bound, then uses binary search to find valid pairs whose sum is within the bound. This approach minimizes redundant calculations and ensures uniqueness by using a set to store results.
